response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm going to be straight with you about this fake content nonsense on social media. I'm on these platforms a few times a week, mainly to keep up with my fitness clients and share workout tips, and the amount of garbage I see is unbelievable. People sharing miracle supplements that'll supposedly transform your body overnight, or bizarre health claims that contradict everything I've learned in my decades as a trainer. It drives me crazy because folks actually believe this stuff. Here's my take on handling it, and I don't care if it sounds harsh because someone needs to say it. First off, use your common sense. If something sounds too good to be true, it absolutely is. I see these posts about losing thirty pounds in a week or building massive muscles without lifting weights, and people share them like they're gospel. Come on now. In my line of work, I know what real results look like, and they come from consistent effort, not magic pills or secret tricks. When I spot obvious fake content, especially about fitness or health, I'll call it out directly. I'm not one to sugarcoat things. My three kids learned early on that Dad doesn't tolerate nonsense, and I approach social media the same way. If someone's spreading dangerous misinformation about exercise or nutrition, I'll tell them they're wrong. Period. Some people get offended, but honestly, protecting people from harmful advice matters more than being polite. The real problem is that most people don't verify anything anymore. They see a headline, get emotional about it, and hit share. I keep my social media time limited precisely because I don't want to get sucked into that chaos. A few times weekly is plenty. When I do encounter questionable content, I'll do a quick search or ask someone knowledgeable before accepting it as fact. It's not complicated. The platforms themselves need to do better too, but I'm not holding my breath waiting for that. At the end of the day, managing fake content comes down to personal responsibility. Don't be lazy about what you believe and share. Question things that seem off. Trust people with actual credentials and experience, not random internet personalities. And if you're going to use social media, at least use your brain while you're at it.
